title: Other Sign Language Scripts and Their Conceptions
subtitle: ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and what they imply about written sign language
summary: Comparative writing-systems paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series.
synopsis: This entry names a few visible other script conceptions as part of the surrounding field. It treats ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, and ASLwrite as distinct answers to different writing problems, and clarifies why SignWriting is understood here as plane-based writing with a broader public and technical path.

slides.title: Why Compare Scripts
slides.summary: Other sign-language scripts clarify the field and prevent SignWriting from being treated as if it exists in isolation.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparative script slide showing ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and SignWriting as distinct approaches to signed-language writing and notation
image_prompts.abstract: Several script conceptions arranged around different writing problems

slides.speech: This paper names other visible script conceptions so SignWriting is not treated as if it exists in isolation. ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and SignWriting answer different problems and make different tradeoffs.

slides.title: Different Conceptions
slides.summary: ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and SignWriting imply different ideas about what signed writing is for.

image_prompts.concrete: Four-column slide comparing ASL-phabet as teaching model, HamNoSys as analytic notation, ASLwrite as script culture, and SignWriting as plane-based writing infrastructure
image_prompts.abstract: Four different answers to the question of written sign language

bullets: SignWriting: plane-based public and technical path
slides.speech: The comparison works because the systems are not trying to do exactly the same thing. ASL-phabet reflects an alphabetic teaching conception. HamNoSys is analytic notation. ASLwrite shows alternate plane-based script culture. SignWriting adds a broader public and technical path.

slides.title: What SignWriting Adds
slides.summary: SignWriting is unusually complete because it links script design, handwriting, encoding, standards, software, datasets, and stewardship.

image_prompts.concrete: Completeness slide showing SignWriting connected to handwriting, encodings, fonts, software, datasets, standards, dictionaries, and stewardship
image_prompts.abstract: A writing system connected to a full infrastructure graph

slides.speech: The SignWriting claim is not that every other script must fail. The narrower claim is that SignWriting currently offers the most developed documented path for durable plane-based signed-language writing with computation, continuity, and public use.

slides.title: Bottom Line
slides.summary: Other scripts are relevant because they sharpen the question: what does each design make easier, and what does it leave for other layers to solve?

image_prompts.concrete: Balanced comparison slide asking what each script design makes easier and what each leaves to other layers
image_prompts.abstract: A comparison lens sharpening several script paths

slides.speech: The bottom line is that other systems should be acknowledged fairly because they clarify the field. The useful question is what each design makes easier and what each leaves for other layers to solve.
slides.notes: Close by returning to the series argument.

slides.title: Comparison Boundaries
slides.summary: The comparison is not a ranking of scripts; it asks what each system is designed to solve and what it leaves to other layers.

image_prompts.concrete: Fair comparison slide showing ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and SignWriting evaluated by purpose, layer, public use, technical path, and open questions rather than a simple ranking
image_prompts.abstract: Several script paths compared through a fair layer-based lens

slides.speech: The comparison is not a simple ranking. The better question is what each system is designed to solve, what layer it works on, what it makes easier, and what it leaves to other layers. That keeps the SignWriting claim specific and keeps other systems visible on their own terms.
slides.notes: Close with fairness. This protects the paper from sounding dismissive while preserving the SignWriting argument.
